narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting whether someone is likely to fail on their first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20, LSAT score of 28, full-time enrollment plans, and school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3—and calculated an 82.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits who show stronger odds of bar passage and professional success. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction, in order of influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the typical student's 5.5—had the strongest negative pull, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers, which strongly raised failure concerns. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, also well under the average of about 5.7, reinforced this pattern of early underperformance relative to others in the dataset. Your LSAT score of 28, much lower than the average of 36.7, added further weight toward the failure prediction by indicating potential challenges with the rigorous reasoning skills needed for bar success. While factors like your full-time status (common among 93% of students) and middle-income bracket aligned with typical profiles and didn't sway things much, they couldn't offset these core academic red flags. Overall, your metrics painted a picture riskier than most applicants, where stronger deciles and LSATs typically tip predictions toward passing. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, while building a stronger academic record through postgraduate courses or targeted prep that could improve future decile-like rankings. Consider community college or targeted tutoring to demonstrate upward trends, then reapply with updated credentials—many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps head-on. This rejection isn't a final verdict on your potential, but a data-driven signal to strengthen these key areas before trying again.
